title: Tick-based Loop Agent: How AI Worked Overnight to Polish Our Demo Site
summary: The Dream-works demo site needed a complete UI/UX overhaul from landing page to individual apps. Instead of manual fixes, we designed a tick-based loop agent that runs autonomously via cron, iterating continuously without supervision. This post dissects the engineering practices behind this 'hands-off development' approach and the problems it exposed.
body:
# Tick-based Loop Agent: How AI Worked Overnight to Polish Our Demo Site

## The Design Was Too Loose

At 4 PM on April 13th, the first version of the dream-works demo site went live. The user took one look and gave blunt feedback:

> 1: This landing page design is too loose, not tight enough; 2: Your generated images should be consistent in size, with image on top and text below to perfectly fit the frame—you didn't achieve this.

Then he made an unusual decision: instead of fixing things line by line himself, he fired up OPC and demanded to "bring together designer, PM, and both new and churned users to systematically solve this problem."

This wasn't bug fixing—this was a redesign. And the user chose a unique way to execute it: tick-based loop agent.

## What Is Tick-based Loop

Starting from chunk 182, a recurring system prompt appeared in the session:

> You are an autonomous execution agent that completes three major improvements to the dream-works demo site through tick-based loop.

Its operating mode:

1. At each tick start, read the state file to understand current progress
2. Decide what to do next
3. Execute one small step
4. Update the state file
5. Wait for the next tick to be triggered by cron

The beauty of this pattern is **resumability**. Each tick is an independent session, unconstrained by context window limits. Even if one tick fails, the next tick can recover and continue from the state file.

## The Truth About Running All Night

From chunk 184 to chunk 194, the session spanned from 4 PM on April 13th to early morning April 14th. With cron triggering every 30 minutes, the AI agent worked continuously for about 8 hours without human supervision.

But the data reveals an uncomfortable truth: chunks 185 to 189 all scored 0.487 with identical preview content—all showing the same tick-based loop system prompt. This means these ticks were likely doing repetitive work or got stuck at some point.

This is an inherent problem with loop agent patterns: **without humans present to course-correct, agents can spin in the wrong direction.** Each tick runs independently, but if the goals in the state file aren't precisely defined, the agent will repeatedly do similar things without knowing it's already done.

## When the Human Came Back

At 10:30 PM on April 13th (chunk 190), the user returned. The score jumped from 0.487 to 0.68, indicating this tick finally made meaningful progress—because it had human input.

The following conversation exposed more problems:

> Should we add .harness-dw to .gitignore—is this necessary? Or should all content be under .harness with different folders? Does this require modifying opc?

While reviewing the agent's overnight output, the user discovered architectural decisions that were never authorized—the agent had autonomously created a .harness-dw directory without anyone approving this naming convention.

This is a core contradiction of autonomous agents: **you want them to be autonomous, but you don't want them making architectural decisions.** Code-level changes (adjusting CSS, fixing layouts) are suitable for autonomous execution; architectural decisions (directory structure, naming conventions) need human approval.

## Dream-works Demo Site's Vercel Journey

Starting from chunk 154, dream-works began its Vercel deployment. This process proved far more complex than expected:

- First was domain chaos: dream-works-smoky, dream-works-app, dreamworks-x, dreamworks-y, dreamworks-z—five domains pointing to different versions
- Then broken links: "I tried dreamworks-x.vercel.app, all the links are fucking wrong"
- Finally Vercel account ownership issues: needing to migrate the project between accounts

Domain cleanup alone took several chunks. The user eventually chose dreamworks-x.vercel.app as the single entry point, pointing three domains (x, y, z) to the same location for verification.

This process seems trivial, but it exposed a deeper issue: when AI rapidly generates and deploys applications, the cognitive overhead of infrastructure management (domains, accounts, CI/CD) grows exponentially. Code can be automated, but DNS configuration and Vercel project setup still require manual human intervention.

## Mass Production of 5 Apps

After organizing the demo site, the session's second half (chunks 214-218) entered an interesting mode: mass producing apps.

The user said: "Let's run those 5 consumer rights apps." The dream-works pipeline was configured to generate and deploy 5 applications in one go. Each app went from idea to Vercel deployment, fully automated.

This was the first real production run since dream-works' unification. Every layer of the pipeline (idea generation → code implementation → UI design → testing → deployment) needed seamless integration.

Starting from chunk 217, the 5 apps were distributed to multiple sub-agents for parallel execution, running until early morning April 15th. This again used tick-based loop pattern, but with lessons learned—more precise state files and clearer completion criteria for each tick.

## Tier 2 Audit: Beyond Just Working

Starting from chunk 251, the user introduced the concept of "Tier 2 audit." Tier 1 is "app runs," Tier 2 is "app is usable"—including accessibility, responsive design, error handling, loading states, etc.

Another round of tick-based loop, another overnight run (chunks 255 to 261, from 5 PM to next day's early morning), this time lasting about 14 hours. But this round's scores were notably more stable (all around 0.64), indicating more effective agent work.

## Retrospective: The Boundaries of Loop Agents

Through this session, I reached several conclusions about tick-based loop agents:

**Suitable scenarios:**
- Clear, verifiable batch tasks (like deploying 5 apps)
- Pure execution work (code changes, test fixes, style adjustments)
- Tasks with clear done criteria

**Unsuitable scenarios:**
- Work requiring architectural decisions
- UI design requiring aesthetic judgment (feedback like "too loose" can only come from humans)
- Exploratory work (when you don't know what the goal is)

**Key improvements:**
- State files must include done criteria, not just progress
- Each tick should have self-assessment: "What meaningful thing did I accomplish this round?"
- Need a "stuck detection" mechanism: if 3 consecutive ticks produce no changes, stop and notify humans

## Conclusion

Tick-based loop agent isn't magic. It's an engineering pattern—using cron + state files + independent sessions to implement a poor man's agentic workflow. It can free humans from grinding, but it can't replace human judgment.

When the user said "landing page is too loose," that feedback had higher information density than all the commits the agent produced in 8 hours combined. The human role isn't executor—it's guardian of taste.
